HRV — heart-rate variability — is the variation in timing between your heartbeats, and it loosely tracks how much recovery capacity your nervous system is showing: more variability generally means more spare capacity. One morning at a given number supports no conclusion in either direction, so here's the conditional read. It's 44 ms today, about 6 ms under your 30-day baseline of 49.8 ms — but your normal day-to-day spread is 5.5 ms, so single mornings this far off happen routinely after a stressful day, a late meal, or a slightly worse night. HRV earns attention through repetition, not single readings: several consecutive days in the low 40s alongside a climbing resting heart rate would be a real downshift signal. For today the honest read is slightly low but within your ordinary range — check again tomorrow before adjusting anything.
